main_summary.summary: Gloria Claudette Grinnell, interviewed April 14, 2013 in Ohio by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program in partnership with the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, recounts her path from a San Francisco and San Diego childhood to participation in the February 1960 Richmond sit-ins as a Virginia Union University student. Her great-great-grandparent helped found Virginia Union, where she enrolled after a West Coast upbringing in the Western Addition and the Presidio that included isolating experiences at St. Dominic's Catholic school. She joined the 34-student Virginia Union sit-in led by Charles Sherrod at Thalhimer's department store in downtown Richmond, was arrested, taken to the Paddy wagon, and held in a segregated courtroom. Her uncle, the Norfolk NAACP attorney J. Hugo Madison, connected her to attorney Martin A. Martin for legal defense. She graduated in 1962, completed a master's at USC and a doctorate in human behavior, taught graduate career psychology at California Lutheran University, and worked as an expulsion counselor for the Los Angeles Unified School District under California Assembly Bill 922. She reflects on Adam Clayton Powell, the March on Washington, the Obama era, and the 2013 Shelby County voting-rights challenge.
key_themes: West Coast Black childhood and migration
main_summary.historical_significance:
Grinnell's testimony documents the Virginia Union University sit-in cohort, one of the earliest wave of student sit-ins (three weeks after Greensboro, February 1960), from the perspective of a participant who arrived from California and experienced Southern segregation as an outsider. Her account names Charles Sherrod as a Virginia Union sit-in leader before his SNCC work in Albany, situates the legal defense within the Virginia NAACP infrastructure (her uncle J. Hugo Madison; attorney Martin A. Martin), and preserves the Norfolk Greyhound bus integration her aunt undertook in coordination with Adam Clayton Powell.

The interview also documents the long-tail employment consequences of a 1960 sit-in arrest record: ten attempts to pass the LAUSD training-and-experience screening, a District of Columbia hiring officer who openly cited her arrest record. Her later expulsion-counselor work at LAUSD provides primary-source observation of racially disparate discipline outcomes for inner-city students versus white students with college trajectories, and her LAUSD textbook-committee account of token Black-history images placed outside chapter content offers a specific instance of curricular tokenism in the post-civil-rights era.

chapters.part: A Family Across the Color Line
chapters.title: They Didn't Hire Black Teachers in Philly
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program on April 14, 2013, Gloria Claudette Grinnell opens with her grandparents, teachers who moved from Virginia to Philadelphia where no one would hire black teachers, her grandfather losing five hundred dollars in the stock-market crash, and her own birth in Washington, D.C. before her parents divorced and her mother took her to San Francisco.

chapters.title: I Hope the Nun Doesn't Slap Me
chapters.summary: Grinnell recalls growing up in the Western Addition and the Presidio, attending St. Dominic's at Pine and Steiner as the only black child, where a nun slapped her during morning prayers and marked all her papers with F's until her mother confronted the teacher, and remembers a white boy refusing to hold her hand and the subtler injuries around her: a teacher reduced to substituting after a dishonorable discharge for refusing to fight in World War II, and her mother caught in a union-and-job catch-22.

chapters.title: I Couldn't Get a Banana Split
chapters.summary: Grinnell tells of arriving in Virginia around 1959 and sitting down at a soda fountain to order an elaborate banana split, only to be refused service because she was black, contrasts the overt Southern segregation with the covert California kind, and explains her mother sent her to Virginia Union though she felt she should have gone to Howard in a city.

chapters.title: Charles Sherrod and the Student Meetings
chapters.summary: She remembers her favorite instructor Dr. Bob Johnson, who held two doctorates and told nervous jokes about being killed driving a white woman home, and recalls student leaders organizing meetings, naming Charles Sherrod, though she cannot recall the substance of the meetings.

chapters.title: Her Father Was Fired and They Moved
chapters.summary: Grinnell describes meeting a fellow sit-in participant at the fiftieth reunion who wept telling how her father was fired and the family had to leave the state, making Grinnell realize how naive she had been to resent Virginia students who did not join, since a sit-in could cost their parents everything.

chapters.title: Am I in Hell
chapters.summary: She recounts being arrested and put in a smelly cell, then seeing the courtroom itself segregated and a drunken man brought before the judge, feeling she had landed in hell, and reflects that laws can change but people change only individually, as she committed to nonviolence though she might have retaliated had she been a man.

chapters.title: Thirty-Four Students at Thalhimer's
chapters.summary: Grinnell describes the thirty-four students sitting in at Thalhimer's department store, where she stood out at over six feet tall in high heels and was refused service in a relatively calm protest, and recalls the jovial mood of walking from school downtown, learning black songs and the black national anthem, and being frightened at an Adam Clayton Powell meeting by a clapping, foot-stamping congregation.

chapters.title: I Was Getting the Hell Out of There
chapters.summary: She explains how her understanding deepened, that black residents had to live there while whites went along with the status quo, but admits she disliked Virginia and was too immature to appreciate it, then recounts leaving for D.C. and a boyfriend before her mother coaxed her back to California, where she spent two restless years partying before moving to Los Angeles.

chapters.title: It Did Hurt Me Job-Wise
chapters.summary: Grinnell describes her career as a California expulsion counselor under Assembly Bill 922 and teaching career psychology to graduate students, then recounts how her sit-in arrest record cost her jobs, a D.C. employer hinting she would not be happy and Los Angeles Unified repeatedly failing her on training and experience until a contract reached her by what she believes was a mistake or a guardian angel.

chapters.title: It Hurts to This Day
chapters.summary: She explains she rarely discusses the sit-ins because she suppressed how uncomfortable they were and it still hurts, partly because attitudes have not changed and black children today are not going to school or voting, and notes that Obama's presidency brought ugly feelings to the surface, recalling her brother who worked behind Henry Kissinger insisting the racial fights were nonsense beside real world problems.

chapters.title: Because His Parents Had Money
chapters.summary: Grinnell recounts writing a three-page letter of protest after a white student deeply involved in drugs was spared expulsion because his parents had money and he was college-bound, while inner-city students who did the same or less were expelled, and reaffirms her elation at Obama's election because he was intelligent and would serve everyone.

chapters.title: Why Are You Only Busing the Black Kids
chapters.summary: She describes serving on a committee during the busing era and being silenced when she asked why only black children were bused, recalls her View Park neighborhood with the top-ranked elementary school full of educators' and professionals' children whose parents refused to bus them, and notes the district pulled every other grade out anyway.

chapters.title: A Black Person at the End of the Chapter
chapters.summary: Grinnell tells how she sent her children to the French school and later public schools like Palisades Charter, declined to prejudice them as her own mother had declined to prejudice her, and recounts serving on a Los Angeles Unified book committee where she objected that a black person was tacked onto the end of a chapter only because the page was torn out for books sent south.

chapters.title: What Is the Civil Rights Movement to You
chapters.summary: Asked what the civil rights movement means to her, Grinnell says young people in Los Angeles care only about brand names, cars, and sports rather than civil rights, and recounts keeping her Los Angeles house in case anyone burned a cross on her Ohio lawn, prompted by a report of a black girl's locker defaced with a slur in Ohio, an overt act that did not surprise her.

chapters.title: We Have Code Words Now
chapters.summary: She describes life in her small town with its expensive boutiques, first-run theaters, horse trails, and private rather than ghetto helicopters, and how counselor home visits taught her to read neighborhoods, then argues the more things change the more they stay the same, racism now covert with code words like a neighbor twice noting she was from a southern state, and that her generation of black professionals, now in their seventies, were funneled into education, social work, ministry, and the post office because those were the only jobs open.

chapters.title: They Weren't Backwards, I Was Backwards
chapters.summary: She recalls a fine graduate experience at USC with few black students and no felt racism, then reflects that her Virginia experience taught her to respect and appreciate different cultures, admitting that at the time she judged Richmond people as backwards when the backwardness was her own, and that the city looked unchanged at the reunion.

chapters.title: You Need to March for Better Jobs
chapters.summary: Grinnell recounts an older black woman at the time asking why the students marched for lunch counters instead of better jobs, a question she dismissed as silly then but came to see differently, notes she graduated in 1962, and recalls being present in Washington for the March on Washington and following the movement while her brother kept her informed of White House strategy meetings.

chapters.title: Let's Take It All the Way Up
chapters.summary: She says she was extremely happy about the civil rights and voting rights acts, arguing that education must come first so people change their thinking and so they vote despite stumbling blocks put in the way, and reacts to the recent challenge to the Voting Rights Act by saying to take it all the way up and see what happens, like her uncle and aunt who loved a good legal fight.

chapters.title: My Uncle Called Me Screwball
chapters.summary: Grinnell recounts how her uncle, who called her screwball, directed her to attorney Martin A. Martin who handled the case, and how her aunt called approvingly from New York, with no one in the family fearful for her, and reflects that her family prepared her without knowing it, passing on values covertly and overtly as her mother's saying held that the squeaky wheel gets attention.

chapters.part: Charles Sherrod and a Catholic Faith
chapters.title: He Was My Boyfriend
chapters.summary: She tells how she reconnected with Charles Sherrod, her college boyfriend and a Baptist minister, after seeing him on a black history broadcast, recounts white parishioners laughing and moving away from her in a Richmond Catholic church until she stopped attending, and remembers going with Sherrod when he interned preaching at emotional country churches that sang with a beat.

chapters.title: I'm Not Too Sure We're That Far From the Past
chapters.summary: Grinnell explains the project moved her to tears because she is unsure how far the present is from the past, having gone online to read the many insults hurled at Obama, weeping over black and white children alike not voting or attending school amid rampant drugs, and admitting she avoids reflecting because thinking of her uncle and aunt's struggles keeps her awake at night.

chapters.part: Testing the Law and Reading the Gut
chapters.title: Call Hugo, I'm Going to Get Arrested
chapters.topic: Her Aunt Integrates the Bus Line
chapters.summary: She recounts how her aunt integrated a Greyhound bus line in Virginia despite the courts having already ruled, insisting on taking the bus, sitting in the white section, getting off to be arrested, and telling her sister to call uncle Hugo and Adam Clayton Powell, illustrating how much testing the law still required even after it was on the books.

chapters.part: Testing the Law and Reading the Gut
chapters.title: The Negro Motorist Green Book
chapters.topic: Navigating Where Black People Could Go
chapters.summary: Grinnell says she still knows which California cities to avoid and trusts her gut over intellect, recalls the Negro Motorist Green Book and how her parents stopped at black homes to find lodging traveling south, where someone would direct them to a Mrs. Jones with a room, and notes that integration shuttered many black businesses, including Peg Leg Bates's upstate New York resort.

chapters.title: Capitalize the Word Black
chapters.summary: Grinnell describes what the black community lost to integration, including children who do not know who they are, and recounts writing the Los Angeles Times and other papers arguing that black should be capitalized because, unlike generic white, it names specifically an American of African descent, and protesting a local paper that called two black women fraudulent with no description, endangering her downtown.

chapters.title: I Guess It Was a Big Deal
chapters.summary: She tells of two white men helping her after she fell from her bicycle and hit her head, taking it as a sign she moves on from slights, then closes by saying she only later understood the Virginia Union sit-in as historic, realizing it when a young librarian asked Dr. Grinnell whether she knew about the civil rights movement and she answered that she had been in it, before insisting the interviewers eat the pound cake she baked.

loc_video.caption: Gloria Claudette Grinnell oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Ojai, California, 2013 April 14
